startScreenOptions.title: AN17.1-AN17.3 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN17.1-AN17.3.

interactions.label: The hip joint is far more stable than the glenohumeral joint of the shoulder. Wh

params.question: The hip joint is far more stable than the glenohumeral joint of the shoulder. Which structural feature of the acetabulum primarily accounts for this difference in stability?

answers.text: The acetabular fossa, which contains a fat pad that cushions the femoral head during weight-bearing

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The acetabular fossa is the non-articular central depression filled with fat and the ligamentum teres. It provides no bony coverage and contributes minimally to joint stability.

answers.text: The deep bony socket augmented by the fibrocartilaginous labrum, covering more than half the femoral head

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The acetabulum provides deep bony coverage over more than half the femoral head, and the labrum adds approximately twenty per cent more depth. This is the key difference — the shoulder's glenoid is far shallower.

answers.text: The transverse acetabular ligament, which bridges the acetabular notch and prevents inferior subluxation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The transverse acetabular ligament bridges the inferior notch and completes the labral ring, but it does not significantly deepen the socket. Stability comes from the depth of bony coverage, not this ligament alone.

answers.text: The crescent-shaped lunate surface, which distributes compressive forces evenly across the joint

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The lunate surface is the hyaline cartilage-lined articular area of the acetabulum. While important for load distribution, it is the depth of the bony socket — not the shape of the articular surface — that provides primary stability.

overallFeedback.feedback: The acetabulum provides deep bony coverage over more than half the femoral head, and the fibrocartilaginous labrum adds approximately twenty per cent more depth. This deep socket is the primary reason the hip is far more stable than the shallow glenohumeral joint.
Correct! The acetabulum covers well over half the femoral head, and the fibrocartilaginous labrum adds about twenty per cent more depth — making the hip the most inherently stable joint in the body.

interactions.label: A patient with hip osteoarthritis reports pain radiating to the medial aspect of

params.question: A patient with hip osteoarthritis reports pain radiating to the medial aspect of the knee, with no local knee pathology. According to Hilton's law, which nerve supplying the hip joint is most likely responsible for this referred pain?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The femoral nerve supplies the anterior capsule of the hip. It refers pain to the front of the thigh, not the medial knee.
The superior gluteal nerve is primarily a motor nerve to the gluteus medius and minimus. It does not supply the hip joint capsule and does not produce referred pain from hip pathology.

The obturator nerve supplies the inferior part of the hip capsule and also innervates the medial compartment of the thigh and knee. Hip pathology therefore commonly refers pain to the medial aspect of the knee via this nerve.
The sciatic nerve supplies the posterior capsule of the hip. It refers pain to the buttock and posterior thigh, not the medial knee.

overallFeedback.feedback: The obturator nerve supplies the inferior hip capsule. Because it also innervates the medial thigh and knee region, hip pathology frequently refers pain to the inner knee — an important clinical pattern explained by Hilton's law.

Correct! The obturator nerve supplies the inferior hip capsule and also innervates the medial thigh and knee — a classic example of Hilton's law explaining why hip disease can masquerade as knee pain.

metadata.title: A patient with hip osteoarthritis report

interactions.label: After total hip replacement via a posterior approach, the patient is counselled

params.question: After total hip replacement via a posterior approach, the patient is counselled to observe specific movement restrictions. Which combination of movements must be avoided to prevent the most common postoperative complication?

answers.text: Hip extension beyond neutral and lateral rotation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Extension and lateral rotation tighten the posterior capsule and actually seat the prosthetic head more securely. These movements do not predispose to posterior dislocation.

answers.text: Hip flexion beyond ninety degrees and internal rotation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Dislocation is the most common complication of THR. In the posterior approach, the short external rotators are detached. Excessive flexion combined with internal rotation levers the prosthetic head posteriorly out of the cup.

answers.text: Active abduction beyond forty-five degrees and external rotation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Abduction opens the joint laterally and does not stress the posterior repair. It is flexion with internal rotation — not abduction with external rotation — that risks posterior dislocation.

answers.text: Full weight-bearing and active hip flexion of any degree

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Early controlled weight-bearing is encouraged after most THRs. The restriction is not on weight-bearing or all flexion, but specifically on flexion beyond ninety degrees combined with internal rotation.

overallFeedback.feedback: Dislocation is the most common complication of total hip replacement. After the posterior approach, the short external rotators are repaired but remain vulnerable. Flexion beyond ninety degrees combined with internal rotation forces the prosthetic head posteriorly, so these movements must be avoided.

Correct! Dislocation is the most common complication of THR. After a posterior approach, flexion beyond 90° combined with internal rotation levers the prosthetic head out posteriorly — hence the strict postoperative precautions.
